en.extract:
The wonga pigeon or wonga wonga (Leucosarcia melanoleuca) is a pigeon that inhabits areas in eastern Australia with its range being from Central Queensland to Gippsland, eastern Victoria, Australia.


Distribution and habitat
Previously they could be found as far north as Cairns and as far south as the Dandenongs, but due to land clearance, shootings in the 1940s for crop protection, and fox predation, they are rarely seen in these areas, but their populations have improved.


Description
The wonga pigeon is a large, plump pigeon that has a short neck, broad wings, and a long tail. Its length varies from 38 to 40 centimetres (15.2 to 16 inches). It has pastel blue-grey back feathers. The head fades to a creamy-white colour. The underside is white with dotted dark grey spots such that a white V can be seen on its chest. Their eyes are a dark red-brown colour and they have pink eye-rings that encircle them. Legs are red and the sexes appear identical but immature pigeons are browner with a less distinct V pattern.


Diet and behaviour
They are very elusive birds, more often being heard but not seen, producing explosive wing claps when disturbed. They tend to occur on the ground foraging and are located in rainforests, wet eucalypt forests, coastal forests, picnic areas, walking tracks, car parks and gardens. Their diet consists of fruit, berries, seeds from native forest trees and the odd insect.


Call
The call of the wonga pigeon is a loud, high-pitched 'coo' repeated over long periods of time for a number of seconds. When males are displaying mating, bowing occurs with a soft, trilling coo.


Breeding
The wonga pigeon is monogamous and breeds between October and January. It builds a twig platform nest with a diameter of about 30 centimetres (12 in), from about 3 to 20 metres (10 to 66 ft) above the ground, defended by breeding pairs. The pigeon will sometimes use abandoned nests from topknot pigeons or tawny frogmouths. Two eggs up to 4 centimetres (1.6 in) long are normally laid.


References

The Wonga Pigeon: Breed Guide - Pigeonpedia

The '''wonga pigeon''' or '''wonga wonga''' ('''''Leucosarcia melanoleuca''''') is a [[dove|pigeon]] that inhabits areas in eastern [[Australia]] with its range being from [[Central Queensland]] to [[Gippsland]], eastern [[Victoria, Australia]].

==Distribution and habitat==
Previously they could be found as far north as [[Cairns]] and as far south as the [[Dandenongs]], but due to land clearance, shootings in the 1940s for crop protection, and [[fox]] predation, they are rarely seen in these areas, but their populations have improved.

==Description==
The wonga pigeon is a large, plump pigeon that has a short [[neck]], broad [[wing]]s, and a long [[tail]]. Its length varies from 38 to 40 centimetres (15.2 to 16 inches). It has pastel blue-grey back [[feather]]s. The head fades to a creamy-white colour. The underside is white with dotted dark grey spots such that a white V can be seen on its chest. Their [[eye]]s are a dark red-brown colour and they have pink eye-rings that encircle them. Legs are red and the sexes appear identical but [[Juvenile (organism)|immature]] pigeons are browner with a less distinct V pattern.

==Diet and behaviour==
They are very elusive [[bird]]s, more often being heard but not seen, producing explosive wing claps when disturbed. They tend to occur on the ground foraging and are located in [[rainforest]]s, wet [[eucalypt]] [[forest]]s, coastal forests, picnic areas, walking tracks, car parks and [[garden]]s. Their diet consists of [[fruit]], [[berry|berries]], [[seed]]s from native forest trees and the odd [[insect]].

==Call==
The call of the wonga pigeon is a loud, high-pitched 'coo' repeated over long periods of time for a number of seconds.<ref>{{cite web |title=Wonga Pigeon |url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Leucosarcia-melanoleuca |website=Xeno Canto |access-date=6 November 2018}}</ref> When males are displaying mating, bowing occurs with a soft, trilling coo.

==Breeding==
The wonga pigeon is monogamous and breeds between October and January. It builds a twig platform [[bird nest|nest]] with a diameter of about {{Convert|30|cm}}, from about {{Convert|3 to 20|m|4=0}} above the ground, defended by breeding pairs. The pigeon will sometimes use abandoned nests from [[topknot pigeon]]s or [[tawny frogmouth]]s. Two [[egg (biology)|eggs]] up to {{Convert|4|cm}} long are normally laid.

zh.extract:
巨地鸠（学名：Leucosarcia melanoleuca），是鸽形目鸠鸽科的一种鸟类，属于巨地鸠属（学名：Leucosarcia）。该属现在是单型的，但若考虑灭绝物种，则另有一个Leucosarcia proevisa De Vis, 1905。


特征
巨地鸠体重约428.57克，翼长约19.70厘米，喙宽度约4.6毫米，喙厚度约4.6毫米，嘴峰长约28.8毫米，跗蹠长约41.2毫米，尾长约13.30厘米。
巨地鸠是留鸟，栖息在森林的树冠层，它们是植食性动物。它们分布在澳大利亚昆士兰州中部至维多利亚州东南部的沿海森林。
